article_title: Astronomers Discover a Planetary System Whose Orbits Are Visibly Changing in Real Time, a Phenomenon Never Before Observed
summary: Well-written science article with three addressable issues: unsourced 'longest-period transiting object' record claim, unsourced star age comparison, and a Triaud quote that doesn't match the Birmingham source.

findings.message: Source fetched via Archive.org fallback (original returned 200)

content_preview.title: Astronomers Discover a Planetary System Whose Orbits Are Visibly Changing in Real Time, a Phenomenon Never Before Observed
content_preview.summary: The TOI-201 system hosts a super-Earth, a warm Jupiter, and a record-setting brown dwarf whose gravitational interplay drives orbital shifts observable on human timescales — within 200 years its planets will stop transiting.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

Astronomers have uncovered a planetary system unlike any other in the known catalog: a three-body system 370 light-years from Earth whose orbits are shifting fast enough to track across a human lifetime. The discovery, published April 15 in *Science Advances*, is rare enough that researchers say it offers a live window into gravitational dynamics that typically play out over millions of years.

The system, designated TOI-201, was identified using a combination of NASA's TESS space t...

editor_notes.content_quality: Excellent science journalism. Well-structured, technically accurate in most respects, good use of researcher quotes and multiple observatory sources. The Significance section adds real editorial value.
editor_notes.source_verification: Fetched all 3 sources. Space.com confirms star is 1.3x mass/size of Sun, 3-planet characteristics (5.85-day super-Earth, 53-day warm Jupiter, ~8-year outer object at 16 Jupiter masses), TESS/ASTEP/LCOGT instruments, 200-year transit cessation prediction, Antarctic advantage for long-period observations. Birmingham confirms ESA Gaia contribution, Concordia Station location, Triaud quotes. Phys.org confirms Mireles and Dragomir quotes verbatim, Science Advances April 15 publication.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: Three issues: (1) 'longest-period transiting object ever discovered' — none of the 3 fetched sources state this specific record claim. It may appear in the academic paper itself but cannot be verified from the cited sources. (2) Star age: the article says the star is 'only about one-tenth its age' (i.e., ~460 million years vs Sun's 4.6 billion) — no fetched source contains a star age comparison. (3) The Triaud quote 'in the act' appears in quotation marks in the article but the Birmingham source has different wording ('a glimpse of what happens shortly after planet formation'). Quotes in quotation marks must match the source verbatim.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral and professional. No sensationalism. Good balance of discovery reporting and uncertainty acknowledgment.
editor_notes.originality: No prior TOI-201 coverage in the archive.
concerns: 'longest-period transiting object ever discovered' is stated as a fact but is not confirmed by any of the 3 cited sources — find the source or remove this record claim
Star age comparison ('only about one-tenth its age') is not in any cited source — add a source or remove
The phrase 'in the act' appears as a direct Triaud quote but does not match the Birmingham source wording — either find the exact quote source or remove quotation marks and make it a paraphrase
recommendations: Verify the 'longest-period transiting object' record claim against the Science Advances paper or a source that states it explicitly
Add a source for the star age comparison or remove the 'one-tenth its age' claim
Fix the Triaud quote: use the actual words from the Birmingham source or rephrase without quotation marks
editor_notes.overall_assessment: Strong science piece with accurate core astronomy. Three easily addressable issues prevent approval. This article will be excellent once corrected.
